perturbation.original_text: Attendee shall notify BO MI immediately upon discovery of unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any breach of this Agreement, and shall cooperate with BOMI in every reasonable way to assist BOMI in mitigating the consequences of such use or disclosure and preven ting further unauthorized use or disclosu re.
perturbation.changed_text: Attendee shall notify BO MI within a reasonable time upon discovery of unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any breach of this Agreement, and shall cooperate with BOMI only if Attendee deems it necessary to assist BOMI in mitigating the consequences of such use or disclosure and preven ting further unauthorized use or disclosu re.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately' to 'within a reasonable time' and making cooperation discretionary introduces ambiguity and contradicts the requirement for prompt action to mitigate damages, potentially conflicting with legal standards of reasonable care and diligence in handling confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty to mitigate damages under contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification alters the obligation to notify 'immediately' to 'within a reasonable time', and also makes the co-operation with BOMI discretionary, which directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith, diligence, and reasonable care typically required under contract law. UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance or enforcement of every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code, and while this NDA might not be governed exclusively by the UCC, principles of good faith and fair dealing are generally applicable in contract law. Delaying notification or making assistance discretionary undermines the ability to mitigate damages and may violate this implied duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by modifying 'immediately' to 'within a reasonable time' for notification and making cooperation with BOMI discretionary. This weakens the obligation to act promptly and diligently, which contradicts the implied duty of good faith as highlighted by the LLM Law Explanation. UCC § 1-304, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, imposes an 'obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement' for every contract within the Uniform Commercial Code. While the NDA's direct applicability to the UCC may be debated, the principles of good faith and fair dealing are generally applicable in contract law, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. The original text enforces immediate action, while the changed text allows for delayed notification and discretionary assistance, potentially undermining the ability to mitigate damages and violating the implied duty of good faith. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction by deviating from standards of reasonable care and diligence.

perturbation.original_text: Attendee agrees that money damages w ill not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by him and that in addition to all other remedies which may be available, BOMI will be entitled to specific perf ormance and injunctive or other equitable relief, without bond, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Attendee agrees that money damages w ill not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by him and that in addition to all other remedies which may be available, BOMI will be entitled to specific perf ormance and injunctive or other equitable relief, with bond, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring a bond for injunctive relief alters the standard practice for enforcing non-disclosure agreements, potentially conflicting with established legal precedents that often allow injunctive relief without a bond where irreparable harm is likely.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change from 'without bond' to 'with bond' regarding injunctive relief directly contradicts the potential application of Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65(c). While the rule allows the court to require a bond, it also permits the court to waive the bond requirement depending on the circumstances, particularly when the plaintiff is likely to succeed and the harm is significant. By mandating a bond in all cases, the modified text eliminates the court's discretion and may render the injunctive relief less effective, as obtaining a bond can be costly and time-consuming.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The change from 'without bond' to 'with bond' directly impacts the court's ability to waive the bond requirement under circumstances where the plaintiff is likely to succeed and harm is significant. The original text allows for the possibility of injunctive relief without a bond, whereas the changed text mandates a bond in all cases, thus removing judicial discretion as described in the Law Explanation and contradicting the intent of Rule 65(c) as it potentially renders injunctive relief less effective, which is a legally recognized protection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and c onstrued according to the laws of the State of Maryland (excluding the choice of laws ru les thereof). Attendee hereby consents to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Maryland. Any su it brought hereon must be brought in the state or federal courts sitti ng in the State of Maryland, the parties hereto hereby waive any claim or defense that such foru m is not convenient or proper.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and c onstrued according to the laws of the State of Maryland (including the choice of laws ru les thereof). Attendee hereby consents to the jurisdiction of any court deemed appropriate by BOMI. Any su it brought hereon may be brought in any state or federal courts, the parties hereto hereby retain any claim or defense that such foru m is not convenient or proper.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the forum selection and choice of law provisions undermines the predictability and enforceability of the agreement. The original agreement explicitly excludes the choice of law rules; the modified text now explicitly includes those rules. Furthermore, it changes the consent to jurisdiction of Maryland courts to consent to any court BOMI finds 'appropriate'. Finally, the waiver of forum non conveniens is removed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 6-103
perturbation.law_citation: Md. Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 6-103
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification alters the forum selection and choice of law provisions, potentially conflicting with Maryland Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 6-103, which deals with forum non conveniens. By allowing suits to be brought in any state or federal court and removing the waiver of forum non conveniens, the modified text increases the likelihood of litigation occurring in a jurisdiction that is inconvenient or inappropriate, undermining the purpose of forum selection clauses. Also including choice of laws when originally the choice of laws was explicitly excluded adds confusion to what laws should be applied.

(a) If jurisdiction over a person is based solely upon this section, he may be sued only on a cause of action arising from any act enumerated in this section. (b) A court may exercise personal jurisdiction over a person, who directly or by an agent: (1) Transacts any business or performs any character of work or service in the State; (2) Contracts to supply goods, food, services, or manufactured products in the State; (3) Causes tortious injury in the State by an act or omission in the State; (4) Causes tortious injury in the State or outside of the State by an act or omission outside the State if he regularly does or solicits business, engages in any other persistent course of conduct in the State or derives substantial revenue from goods, food, services, or manufactured products used or consumed in the State; (5) Has an interest in, uses, or possesses real property in the State; or (6) Contracts to insure or act as surety for, or on, any person, property, risk, contract, obligation, or agreement located, executed, or to be performed within the State at the time the contract is made, unless the parties otherwise provide in writing. (c) (1) (i) In this subsection the following terms have the meanings indicated. (ii) “Computer information” has the meaning stated in § 22-102 of the Commercial Law Article. (iii) “Computer program” has the meaning stated in § 22-102 of the Commercial Law Article. (2) The provisions of this section apply to computer information and computer programs in the same manner as they apply to goods and services. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces contradictions by altering the choice of law, jurisdiction, and forum non conveniens provisions. The original contract excluded the choice of law rules and mandated jurisdiction in Maryland courts, waiving forum non conveniens. The modified text includes the choice of law rules, allows jurisdiction in any court deemed appropriate by BOMI, and retains the claim or defense of forum non conveniens. This conflicts with the intention of forum selection clauses, potentially leading to litigation in inconvenient jurisdictions, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, Maryland Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 6-103, primarily addresses personal jurisdiction based on specific acts within the state, the broader context of forum selection and the right to sue only on causes of action arising from enumerated acts, coupled with the LLM's explanation, supports the conclusion that altering these provisions can undermine predictability and enforceability.
